definition: Occult means hidden — and, since the 17th century, the hidden *arts*: magic, astrology, alchemy, and their kin. An *occultist* practices them.
history: Latin *occultus*, 'concealed' (past participle of *occulere*, 'to cover over'). Astronomers still say a moon *occults* a star when it passes in front and hides it — the occult is simply 'the hidden' before it was 'the sinister.' The sense 'supernatural arts' sharpens around the 1630s, and 'occultist' is a Victorian coinage, arriving with the 19th-century magical revival.

def_sources.label: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
def_sources.abstract: Traces how modern Wicca -- the pagan witchcraft religion that emerged in 20th-century England -- was actually assembled by blending older folklore, ceremonial magic, and Romantic ideas about nature, rather than being an unbroken survival of ancient paganism.

commentary: 'The occult' as a unified field is younger than it looks. Wouter Hanegraaff has shown that 'Western esotericism' was assembled as a scholarly category only recently, gathering astrology, alchemy, magic, and Kabbalah into one object of study that earlier centuries did not treat as a single thing. The 19th-century coinage 'occultism' did comparable work. To speak of 'the occult sciences' is already to take a position in a debate about what belongs together.

commentary_sources.label: Wouter J. Hanegraaff, Esotericism and the Academy
commentary_sources.abstract: Explains why universities have historically dismissed astrology, alchemy, and magic as "not real knowledge," and argues that this rejection says as much about how scholars built their own identity as it does about the actual content of those traditions.

commentary_sources.label: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
commentary_sources.abstract: Traces how modern Wicca -- the pagan witchcraft religion that emerged in 20th-century England -- was actually assembled by blending older folklore, ceremonial magic, and Romantic ideas about nature, rather than being an unbroken survival of ancient paganism.

etymology.origin: Latin occultus ‘hidden, concealed’, pp. of occulere ‘to cover over’
etymology.note: Astronomers still say a moon ‘occults’ a star — passes in front and hides it. The occult is simply ‘the hidden,’ before it was ‘the sinister.’

mechanics_snippets.html:
Imoen, <mark>Occult</mark> Trickster can't be blocked.
Whenever Imoen deals combat damage to a player, you may exile an instant or sorcery card from your graveyard. If you do, create a 2/2 black Zombie creature token.
